narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 52.6% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold that leads to rejection for many applicants. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 29, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 3 and 2 respectively, your lower-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27, undergraduate GPAs average 3.24, and Year 1 and Year 3 deciles are typically around 6—showing your metrics are below those benchmarks. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, as lower rankings like yours strongly signal challenges in sustaining strong performance, which has been a key hurdle for similar students. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 3, which also pulled the prediction toward failure by highlighting an early academic struggle compared to passers' higher averages. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually nudged the prediction toward success, since while most bar passers come from middle to upper-middle income backgrounds, your bracket has still produced some strong outcomes. Your full-time enrollment plan aligns well with the 93% of passers who chose that path, offering a subtle boost. Overall, these top three elements—dominated by your decile rankings—outweighed the positives and led to the rejection. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially as a full-time aspiring lawyer with a solid undergraduate foundation, but this isn't a judgment on your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim for 35 or higher, bolstering your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Many students in similar positions have improved their profiles and succeeded on later attempts—reapplying after targeted improvements could shift your odds dramatically.
